title: The swap
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: The console shows two identical numbers. They were supposed to be swapped, not identical.
why.not-closed: A tag in index.html is open and never closed.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below.
why.typed-answer: You typed the number by hand instead of swapping.
hint2.typed-answer: The eight and the twelve appear only on the first two lines, where the names are created.
hint3.typed-answer: Tomorrow the shifts will be 40 and 55. If you typed the answer by hand, your code will lie. The swap has to work with any numbers.
hint2.no-third: Two names are not enough. One of the values has to be kept somewhere before it gets overwritten.
hint2.morning-wrong: The first line of the swap destroys the old value of morning. It has to be saved before that.
hint3.morning-wrong: Think of two cups with different liquids. You cannot swap them by pouring directly. You need a third, empty one.
hint4.morning-wrong: Create a third name, give it the value of morning, then swap the two, and finally take back what you saved.
hint2.evening-wrong: The eight has disappeared somewhere along the way. Check which line overwrote it.
why.wrong-count: There are not two lines in the console.
why.lost-p: A line has disappeared from the page.
blocks.0.text: The morning shift sold 8 cups, the evening shift 12. It turns out the numbers were swapped and have to change places.
blocks.1.text: The code in script.js does exactly that. Or so it looks. The console shows 12 and 12.
blocks.3.text: The first line says: morning now points at whatever evening points at. That is, at 12.
blocks.4.text: And at that moment the eight is already gone. Nothing is holding it. Nothing points at it.
blocks.5.text: The second line says: let evening point at whatever morning points at. And morning already points at 12. So both become 12.
blocks.7.text: The equals sign in maths says the two sides are the same thing. In code it says something else: take the right-hand side and make the left-hand one point at it.
blocks.9.text: Here a becomes 2. But b does not become 1. It only goes one way.
blocks.11.text: Until now the lines in your code could be shuffled without consequence. From here on they cannot.
blocks.12.text: Every line changes the state, and the next one works on the changed version. That is also why code is hard to read — to understand line 40 you need to know what the first 39 did.
blocks.14.text: Swap the two values. Afterwards morning should be 12 and evening should be 8.
blocks.15.text: The numbers 8 and 12 appear only where the names are created. The swap has to work even if the numbers change tomorrow.
